data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A series of unprecedented winter storm surges between 1220‑1180 BC inundated coastal lowlands, salinizing soils and destroying agricultural productivity across Mycenaean Greece, Hittite Anatolia, Ugarit, and Levantine city‑states.", "mechanism": "Hydro‑climatic regime shift toward more intense cyclonic activity caused recurrent flooding and salt‑water intrusion, leading to crop failure and famine.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city‑states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "High‑resolution lake sediment cores from the eastern Mediterranean show no increase in flood‑derived deposits during 1220‑1180 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This hypothesis predicts a temporal correlation between storm‑derived sediment layers and archaeological signs of abrupt abandonment; it diverges from hyper‑arid drought models by emphasizing excess water rather than scarcity."}
